title: Andie Vorwerk -Hiring Manger Interview - Operations | Account Strategist
meeting_title: Andie Vorwerk -Hiring Manger Interview - Operations | Account Strategist

transcript.text: Hey, how are you?
This meeting is being recorded.
Good.
So few other Andi ladies.
I got excited when he said that.
It was so exciting.
I actually worked with another female Andi a few years back, so that's funny.
Love it.
Amazing.
Yeah, I've worked with several Baileys, and everybody's like, wow, but this is way more fun.
Absolutely.
Well, thank you for scheduling time.
I'm sorry I'm a little bit late, but I figure I can start by giving you a little bit of context on myself, growthx, and how we're thinking about the role.
I know you've already talked to Tucker, so this may be some repetition, but hopefully not too much.
We could talk about you, and then I try to leave at least 10 minutes at the end to ask questions, so pretty standard.
But, yeah, anything else?
Thank Thank
Any other questions on the process?
Okay, great.
Let's jump in.

So I lead customer operations for GrowthX, which basically just means I'm overseeing kind of like all the process and organizational pieces that go into getting our deliverables out to clients or for clients.
And I don't know how much research you've done on like GrowthX as a whole, but we are essentially like a marketing content agency.

We operate under a services model, but everything that we're doing is generated off of our own internal platform that's leveraging like different AI tools throughout a workflow to put out content for clients.
And we are able to do that like at a much more efficient scale than like traditional agencies.
So the way that kind of our pods are set up is we have content managers who are in the platform data.
A running workflows, have managing editors who are sort of project managers and literal editors for the content that we're generating.
They're also kind of helping coordinate tickets and feedback on the platform itself.
And then an account strategist, this role is kind of like shown up in different ways in our one year existence.
But I think the way that we're thinking about it right now is, I mean, it's in the title, right?

So somebody that is thinking long term about the success of the partnership, helping lay the tracks kind of ahead of where the CM and ME are on a daily basis, helping like align deliverables to clients' long term goals.
So a lot of like traditional sort of CSM account management aspects, but with that keeping in mind.
Kind of the, A, like the marketing of it all, like the industry, services industry piece, which is a little bit different than like SaaS, than supporting SaaS.
And then the other side is like, because we're leveraging an internal platform, our pods are basically like UX, like they are the like UX feedback loop.

And so the account strategist is also somebody who's thinking about like quality of output, and how we improve like both the feedback loop and the like tool work stream deliverables themselves to make their pods more effective.
So you're kind of a function of the ops piece too, just because we're so small.
So I need somebody that's kind of unable to understand each piece of the process and help make it better as we're going.
So, um, sorry.
you want to break the next question?
Yeah.
Yeah, was just about to say.
What does the pod consist of?
Like what, who all is in the pod?
A content manager, a managing editor, and an account strategist.
And so I guess like account strategist oversees two to three pods and a managing editor has three to five accounts.
So the managing editor is kind of like the account, the day-to-day account, like not owner, but like they're the day-to-day account point of contact.
Account strategist is like high-level account owner.
So you're owning eight to 10 accounts.
You have two managing editors who are sort of like the core of their two pods.
I guess we should have a different word for like the account pod and then the managing editor pod.
But at this point we don't.
So we have pods, mini pods, then the mini pods are like the managing editor, and then the content managers have like one to two accounts.
So a managing editor.
That has three or more accounts, we'll have usually have two content managers.
Okay.
It's classic pyramid structure, but yeah.
So like eight accounts overall, and then you will have like four to seven people supporting the day-to-day deliverables of the accounts that you manage.
Okay, got it.
And then for the day-to-day deliverables, that falls under account strategies or no?
So the content managers and managing editors are generating the day-to-day deliverables.
And you're kind of just like, you'll be in the Slack channels making sure things are on track.
You are a point of escalation.
You're joining client calls, which are weekly.
And just like kind of keeping an eye on like analytics and making sure that we're still.
Answers are Reddit, as in it's an open forum.
So think pre-ChatGPT.
This was where you would definitely go if you're learning how to code.
If you get stuck on a coding question, you know, we have a team of people that are vetting the community, the answers.
It's basically like a vetted Reddit specifically for coding questions.
To say that.
So, you know, at any given point in time, we have about 100 million subscribers per month.
So sell banner ads internally on the site, all vetted to technology companies.
So, you know, our big players are Azure, Google, AWS.
We also have, you know, we have people across the board, but anyone who's trying to get in front of that developer audience is who we advertise to.
So I work with the clients that manage the banner ads.
So I work with a lot of agencies.
And then on the flip side, I actually work with our SaaS product called Collectives, which is.
It's basically a mini stack overflow that's targeted to the client's questions on the site.

So Azure is my client, they have their own collective, and that's the space where you can go, you know, learn about Azure, and all the questions are going to be there in one spot, and then there's also going to be like announcements and brand engagement and things like that.
So is your account management for that more on, like, the product side, or are you helping with, like, content strategy and things like that in terms of what they're servicing?
More on the product side, so more just making sure that, like, the analytics are going well.
So I don't, yeah, so I'm not in the content creation at all, but we are helping engage as much as possible.
So we talk about community engagement, we talk about the analytics and building a story from the analytics and optimizing based on that.
So that's a big part of that.
And then kind of the same goes for the ad side, a little different because we don't create the
But we are working with that and creating strategies together, working on targeting.
We actually have, like, I should know this answer, like 40 or 50 sites that aren't Stack Overflow.
So we have, it's called Stack Exchange, and we have a different set of sites that we run on.
And so kind of targeting those sites based on the product that they're selling and, you know, what they're promoting at that time.
So we have overarching strategy.
We, it's basically our own ad strategy within the world of Stack Overflow.
Yeah.
Okay, cool.
Well, what, like, results are you guys tracking when you're, you know, what, what is the, like, success metric that clients are looking at for your?
It is client dependent.
So my, my big client is, they're tracking acquisitions.
So we track cost per acquisitions and they actually share their back-end data with us for us to get that full.
while Yeah.
So it's actually a combo of our data and their data, which is, I don't want say better, but it's been a more effective way to go about things.
We've definitely found that the deeper the partnership with our clients, the better strategies we can make just based off of what they're sharing.
The bigger the picture we get, the better we can kind of build that strategy out.
Yeah, that's something that we see a lot too.
I'm just taking notes.
Yeah, of course.
And I know Stack operates kind of a little differently than most companies.
So I get that that can be a little like curve volley, just.
No, but that's, I mean, that's, it's a good thing for us.
Because I think like, we're also, our model is also a little bit different, right?
It's like a combo of like SaaS and services.
Like we're selling the SaaS.
But you don't get to access it.
It's actually like the service.
We're selling the service, which is access to our product.
Yeah, back end.
Yeah, which is interesting.
Like, I don't think I fully understand that until I don't on this call.
Like, I don't think that fully clicked into place.
Yeah, so there is a world eventually where I think we will surface the product to clients.

But like the way that Marcel built out the company, our CEO built out the company was like, he first was doing like webinars, which were like, look at how I'm leveraging AI to like put out all this content.
And then he, people went to the webinar and were like, this is really complicated.
Can you just do it?
Yeah, I don't want to do this.
You're using like 13 different AI tools, and like connecting them all.
And this is just confusing.
Like, can you just do it?
So then he paired up with an engineer, like to co found the company.
And they built out workflows using like the processes that Marcel had.
And then they were like, okay, now we're going to build it into our own platform because they were doing it on AirOps.
And then they transitioned to like build it on their own so that we could, you know, have a little bit more flexibility and customization for what we wanted to do, what he wanted to do.
So yeah, it's like a little bit of both.
So it's good that you're like coming from a bit of a different background.

We also like, because we're like, we're ingesting all of everything from the client, and then like, giving an output back that's supposed to like represent the client, we have gone very hard on like, give us access to all of your analytics, like, give us your call recordings, what is your like most successful sales call, like, who's your ICP, like, give us everything that you have, like support tickets that are like, that we want to talk about.
So that makes a lot of sense.
So it's like, and I think on our end, like, yeah, clients are defining their results totally differently.
For us, it's almost a benefit because we don't have like a unified dashboard.
So we're starting to figure out where everybody's going.
But it's, yeah, like to be able to have that conversation is certainly a positive.
Absolutely.
I do think it's interesting too, like knowing that you're in kind of a client by client basis.
It helps a little bit with actually, like, in my opinion, with like account management, like CS type of jobs, just because you know, like, you know, that's not cookie cutter.
We're going to go in there and we're going to actually like, you have to do some discovery and figure out like what's important.
Because sometimes they think they know what, they tell you what they think is important.
And like two months later, you're like, you don't care about this.
You care about that.
Yeah.
Yeah.
How do you think about organizing across clients when everybody's like a little bit unique and it's not scaled?
In that same way, like, how do you prioritize?
How do you just like, how does your organization, your brain work in terms of organizing clients?
I'm definitely like a timing prioritized person.
In my personal life, I'm a procrastinator.
I'm not in my professional life, but my friends laugh because there is a little overflow.
They're like, you just figure out what needs to be done and do that.
And then from there, you prioritize everything else.
And work, I'm a little bit like that.
Just not so much the procrastinating, but as far as prioritizing goes, you know, sometimes you get an email and you're like, this is so low priority.
I'm putting it on the list, but it's not, it doesn't need to be done or that needs to be done by next Friday.
And sometimes you get something and you can just tell like, okay, this is important and we need to pull all the players and we need to get this done right this second.
So I think, um, it's one managing.
I don't know how you guys are sold.
I don't know if you have bigger clients versus smaller clients.
I know for me, my book of business, I prioritize my biggest clients.
For reference, my biggest client at any given point in time is running $2 million worth of ads, and my next client is running $100,000.
So like, you know, if, if Azure is asking for something, they say jump, yeah, that's the priority.
Most, most of the time, that's not always totally true.
But pretty much, if it's them versus them, you know, you gotta you gotta go with the long term financial goals there.
But I do think that being said, that's not to say that this $100,000 client can't grow into a $2 million client.
Because we we always, there's so much potential there, I think.
So I think it's just taking that into consideration.
And then also looking at the requests, like knowing, knowing what needs to be done, and knowing your job all enough that you can prioritize based off.
Off of what needs to be done, the timely manner.
Yeah.

Can you talk me through, like, a project or something where you got that sort of, like, urgent request and you had to, like, build something through or, like, see through kind of a deliverable from end to end?
Yes.
So we don't really do deliverables.
Like, we do.
Right.
Or, like, requests.
Like, you had to respond to requests immediately.
You had to pull people in.
Like, sort of.
Yeah.
So, shocking.
I'm going to talk about Azure.
They're my baby.
They've been my client for four years.
So it's just, like, those are my guys.
But I have a story, but it doesn't really answer your question.
So.
No, go ahead.
We'll start there and then I'll ask for questions.
Yeah.
So I just like to talk about, like, the partnership that I've built with them and then I can actually.
Should we talk about something that happened this week?
didn't have the background with them.
So when I joined, we were running ads, and the way that we had this account set up was insane, in my personal unbiased opinion.
We were meeting with them like two to three times a week, which is fine if that's what needs to happen, but it was a little bit of overkill.
And it really wasn't good meetings.
It was because they didn't really trust us to be running the way that they wanted to run.
And they didn't trust us to deliver.
We were having issues across the board just on getting their campaigns to deliver what they bought.
And it was a mess.
So when I got in, I was like, why is anything set up like this?
This is crazy.
So it kind of just took a big picture, stepped back.
And I also was the new kid.
So I got the team together.
And I was like, this is how I understand we should be running it.
Why are we not running it like this?
And everyone was just like, this is just how we have always done.
So there wasn't a good, there wasn't anything.
So I essentially got on with the client was just like, I'm gonna be really transparent with you, I would like to apologize, I don't think we're doing this right.
And I'd like to completely change how we're doing it.
And so we essentially had to reset up all the campaigns, and they're running 54 campaigns a quarter, so not a small lift.
And I can tell you that my pod that I was working with was not thrilled with me.
But essentially, we got that big lift, we made it start running like a normal campaign, immediately, they saw ROI and like better CPAs, which is their KPI for that.
And we, we just built up that trust.
And I think that they kind of really respected that I just came in and was like, you know what, we're doing this wrong, and we're going to shift it.
And now they still run the same amount with us, we only meet once a month.
And it's not because they don't want to talk to us, it's because they just trust us.
And when we
We to jump on a call in the interim.
We absolutely will.
But it's kind of just building up that trust.
But also, to your point, like knowing when to pull people in and knowing when to be like, listen, this is if we do this, then we have less work to do weekly.
Yeah.
And, and the client's going to trust us and they're going to come back and spend more money.
Amazing.
That's, I mean, that's a good example.
I don't know where you were to start.
I mean, like, I pulled in that.
Yeah, you got me pulled in your answer, but that's not usually, that's my go-to when you fixed a problem.
Yeah.

Okay.

Um,

So, oh, does that make sense?
Because they want us all be like the value, your move around based on value, not matched with a salesperson on a book, but we were matched with sales.

But at that point in time, I was only having Microsoft, because he, that's all he does.

20 something clients and you have the thing.

So I do have multiple salespeople that I work with, but my operations is the same person every time.
Yeah.

We work a little bit with product marketing and a little bit with the engineering team, like support team, but not when I say like a little bit, like I talk to them once every six weeks type of thing.
Okay.

That's.

Developer relations.

Meetings and talking about strategy overall, like, that's.

Like, yeah.

Okay.

I still have a job.

And I have just kind of feel like I've plateaued here.

to go professionally.
So I'm just spinning my wheels, it feels like.

So that's pretty much what I'm looking for.
And just a new challenge.
Yeah.
Yeah.

Like, are you using it at all?
Are you using it in your processes?

And yeah, what role did you play in the agency?

I owned the deliverables.
I owned the timelines, you know, the internal processes.
So like the request comes in, I owned the budget for those.
And that would, that was essentially what we did for United Landmark Associates, which I was at for two years.

Like kind of like a niche spot to be in.
But those budgets were a lot bigger, which means we had a lot more to do with it.
And then.

And we would do anything from digital marketing to like fence wraps, like, you know, when you drive by a construction site, and there's, and then we also would work with the rendering companies, we would work with the interior designers, we'd have lookbooks made.

A little bit.
A little bit.

I can understand it.

So I know, again, surface level, talking about it, and I just wouldn't say I'm, like, a content writer that could, like, help with SEO, necessarily.
Yeah, yeah.

What questions do you have for me?
So I saw that you, you're pretty new to this role, right?
Yeah.
Three months.
Yeah.
So how do you feel like that's been going?
Like, how do you feel about the company, and, like, what have you walked into?
Um, I think it feels a lot longer than three months.
Like, we're such a young company, and, and there is, like, there's so much to do that, like, if you walk
And you're like, oh , like there's, you know, if you can just pick something up and start doing it and running with it, like there's so much space to execute.
And like, you know, if you have an idea and like want to change something, there's room to do that.
So I feel like I've been given a lot of like, not leeway, but like opportunity for ownership.
So it feels a lot longer than three months just because I sort of dove in.
Um, I think we're getting better at our internal training on like processes and stuff.
Our platform is two months old.
So like also brand new.
Um, so, you know, we're changing how we're doing things at the same time as like I'm learning.
So I think like even as a new person, there's so much to learn.
And that's changing both like in the space writ large and within growth X for like the product.
that we're using, and how we're leveraging AI, and like, you know, that I think like, very quickly, you don't feel like a newbie, but you have to feel like an owner.
Absolutely.
Well, that's in startups, I feel.
In my experience with small companies and startups, it's definitely, you're either in it or you're not.
Yeah.
So, how many, like, how many people are on the team right now?
And like, do you have any account strategists actively right now?

So, we have someone that came from a product ops background, and he was like, supposed to be in, he was supposed to like, manage product operations, and then he kind of got pulled into like, helping with implementation.
And he became the model for what we're calling an account strategist.
He's sort of like become our chief of staff, like, you know, his role just keeps changing.
But when he was helping out with this one piece, it was so impactful that we were like, we need another George.
In fact, we need two more Georges because he like, but he was essentially doing like implementation CS work to stand up accounts and make sure that like we were setting strategies.
And like had a path forward on deliverables and then like could hand it off to steady state teams.
think there's also a world where this person just is like not just implementation, but like becomes like manages a book of accounts of like eight to 10 accounts.
So yeah, it is a new role in that like we hadn't, we didn't like come up with it and then say like, this is.
Oh.
Person zero, but like somebody started doing the job and then we were like, we need more people doing this job.
He was closing a gap.
And we do have account directors today.
But their experience is really like deeply in internal marketing, like they have worked in house at companies before as like a marketing specialist as like on the marketing team.

And so like, we were looking for people more with like an account management background to help, you know, so we do have that expertise in house now that can help with like those like SEO and all that stuff.
But the account management pieces is something we're really focused on for now.
Okay, awesome.
And then like, out of curiosity, do you guys have like any focus on like, industries or company size?
Or are you kind of all over the map?

With like series BC type companies, We don't, but like industry, I'll open the map.

And those like household names, security and compliance customers.

We can build on that.
They have a clear idea of their own like audience and persona.
And then we can start to like refine.
And like, if they have built the foundations, we can scale it and like, just run with that stuff.
If they're starting from zero and like are a super scrappy org that hasn't put out any content really, like the results just.
Like
It takes so long to build from zero that a lot of companies get like impatient waiting.
It's like, well, you didn't have any content.
What did you expect?
Yeah, there's nothing for the AI to build off of there.
Yeah, that's interesting.
But I also see where they would just want to offload that.
Do you guys do?
Yeah.
Okay, yeah, no, that makes total sense.
Do you, so you're still kind of building this rollout.
So there's probably not like a real answer for like, career projection or anything there.
there?

B, how quickly we grow and acquire clients, like, you know, rising tide lifts all boats sort of thing.

So, like, hire teams and sort of grow in that way.

I wouldn't really expect there to be.

Yeah.
I mean, I can say, like, we reward, like, good performance and stuff like that from, like, a compensation standpoint.
But, so, like, and that's in less than a year of existing, we've been able to, like, spot bonuses or things like that.
But, you know, we have been around for a year, so we don't have, like, a clear.
But I like that.
I mean, I think that's something that's really interesting about, like, this job and where you guys are at is that you can come in and be like, okay, this is working really well.
And this maybe isn't working so well and is there room to change it?
Because I think that that's something that we're, I know we're missing right now where I am.
There's some issues.
And like, every time someone proposes something, they're like, well, maybe it might get implemented in eight months.
And I'm like, I forgot that we even suggested this.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So I think that that's like a really unique position to be in, and a good one.
And then just out of curiosity, like, what is like the one thing that you're looking for in this role?
Like, if you had to say like the most important thing for the account strategist, what would you say?
Or is that impossible?
No, I think like a ownership mindset, and the, like, get it done, get things out the door.
And if it means that you are the person that has to go into like our platform and run the workflows on some days, like that might be what happens.
Like, we have people out in the next two weeks.
And so like, you know, everybody has to do A scrappy.
And a willingness to, like, get your hands dirty when you need to, and, like, own the outcome.
Yeah, absolutely.
That makes total sense.
I also believe that you just need that in any remote environment as well.
Oh, yeah, for sure.
Oh, my gosh.
Are you guys fully remote, right?
There's no office, or is there?
There is an office in San Francisco.
I think four people sit in it.
I was there last week, you know, like, they were like, Like, it's so busy.
It's like, there's six people here right now.
Like, yeah.
Where are you located?
I'm in Rhode Island.
What about you?
I'm in Tampa, Florida.
cool.
Nice.
He's a ghost going on, but I know people like San Francisco, so that's about where, like, the CEO is.
Yeah, he's in San Francisco.
Yeah, that makes sense.
Yeah.
Okay.
Okay.
Okay.
Okay.
Okay.
Okay.
I think those are all my, all my main questions.
Do you have anything else that I can answer for you?
No, I don't think so.
Okay.
Awesome.
Well, thanks for your time, Andi.
It was great to meet you.
Yeah, it's been a pleasure.
Thank you.
Bye.

## Meeting Purpose

[Interview with Andi Bailey for Account Strategist role at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/mYEwt1s5iibgx3SqQXVMpBzHKEfQTsnz?tab=summary&timestamp=30.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX is a marketing content agency leveraging AI tools to produce content efficiently at scale](https://fathom.video/share/mYEwt1s5iibgx3SqQXVMpBzHKEfQTsnz?tab=summary&timestamp=80.0)
  - [Account Strategist role involves long-term partnership strategy, aligning deliverables to client goals, and providing platform/UX feedback](https://fathom.video/share/mYEwt1s5iibgx3SqQXVMpBzHKEfQTsnz?tab=summary&timestamp=151.0)
  - [Andi has 4 years of experience at Stack Overflow in ad sales and account management for major clients like Microsoft Azure](https://fathom.video/share/mYEwt1s5iibgx3SqQXVMpBzHKEfQTsnz?tab=summary&timestamp=1000.0)
  - [Andi is seeking a new challenge with AI integration and more growth opportunities](https://fathom.video/share/mYEwt1s5iibgx3SqQXVMpBzHKEfQTsnz?tab=summary&timestamp=1449.0)

## Topics

  - [Team structure: Content Managers, Managing Editors, Account Strategists](https://fathom.video/share/mYEwt1s5iibgx3SqQXVMpBzHKEfQTsnz?tab=summary&timestamp=111.0)
  - [Account Strategist oversees 2-3 pods (8-10 accounts total)](https://fathom.video/share/mYEwt1s5iibgx3SqQXVMpBzHKEfQTsnz?tab=summary&timestamp=277.0)
  - [Responsible for long-term strategy, aligning deliverables to goals, quality control, platform feedback](https://fathom.video/share/mYEwt1s5iibgx3SqQXVMpBzHKEfQTsnz?tab=summary&timestamp=206.0)

### Andi's Current Role at Stack Overflow

### GrowthX Company Culture and Growth
